title: Autonomous Spatial-Detection Rover
subtitle: Embedded robotics project integrating sensing, motion control, and rapid prototyping.
alt: Small tracked engineering rover with sensors, control board, wiring, and 3D printed components.
role: Mechanical integration, wiring, programming, and testing
tools: Python / embedded controls, sensors, servos, 3D-printed fixtures
challenge: Build a compact mobile robot capable of detecting nearby objects and reacting to its environment rather than following only a fixed path.
approach: Integrated sensor inputs with motor and servo control, organized the exposed electronics into a functional package, and iterated the detection logic through repeated classroom testing. The orange printed bracket supported the sensor arrangement while keeping the system accessible for adjustments.
outcome: Produced a working rover that demonstrated spatial detection, controlled motion, and the interaction between mechanical layout and software behavior.
learned: Sensor placement can matter as much as the code interpreting the signal.
Fast physical access to wiring and components makes testing and iteration much more efficient.
Small mechanical changes can noticeably improve repeatability in autonomous behavior.

title: Sherpa Cleaning Drone Production
subtitle: Electromechanical assembly and troubleshooting for an industrial exterior-cleaning platform.
alt: Open industrial cleaning drone showing wiring, power electronics, circuit boards, frame components, and 3D printed protective parts.
role: Robotics Engineering Technician at LUCID Bots
tools: Mechanical assembly, wiring, production testing, troubleshooting, quality checks
challenge: Support reliable production of a complex cleaning drone that combines a carbon-fiber frame, motors, power electronics, communications hardware, sensors, and fluid-delivery components.
approach: Built full systems from mechanical subassemblies through wiring, setup, inspection, and final testing. Diagnosed fit, routing, damaged-component, and quality-control issues while communicating recurring concerns to engineering and production leads.
outcome: Helped move the team from a backlog of roughly 50 units toward on-time production while gaining hands-on experience with complete electromechanical systems.
learned: Reliable production depends on consistent routing, fastening, inspection, and documentation.
Recurring build problems should be surfaced as process-improvement opportunities, not treated as isolated fixes.
A complete-system viewpoint helps connect individual assembly steps to final product performance.

title: Parametric Skid and Revit Coordination
subtitle: Turning 2D project information into coordinated, buildable model content.
alt: Three-dimensional Revit coordination model of a modular electrical skid structure with equipment and routed systems.
role: College Project Engineer Intern, Virtual Design and Construction
tools: Autodesk Revit, AutoCAD, Bluebeam Revu, Autodesk Construction Cloud, Evolve
challenge: Translate project drawings and design information into accurate Revit content that could support coordination, shop drawings, installation planning, and modular fabrication.
approach: Built and revised model content from AutoCAD files, project prints, and existing plan information. Supported configurable Revit family development for skid and mounting components while checking dimensions, installation constraints, and constructability.
outcome: Created clearer, repeatable model information for design and field teams while helping identify coordination questions before installation.
learned: Parametric families are most valuable when their controls match real fabrication and installation decisions.
Model accuracy must be paired with drawing review and field awareness.
Good VDC work reduces ambiguity between design intent and physical construction.

title: Custom Skid Lift Planning
subtitle: Engineering calculations connected directly to a successful field lift.
alt: Custom modular skid suspended from crane rigging during a field lift with workers observing from a safe distance.
tools: Drawing review, component data, weight estimates, load-path reasoning, field coordination
challenge: Support the safe movement of a custom modular skid by translating model and component information into practical lift-planning inputs.
approach: Used project dimensions and equipment information to support expected-weight calculations and lift assumptions, then compared the planned configuration with the actual field setup before observing execution.
outcome: Connected analytical work to a real construction operation and saw the custom skid lifted into position as planned.
learned: Field calculations must be traceable to drawings, equipment data, and clearly stated assumptions.
A technically correct result still needs to be communicated in a form the project team can use.
Watching the lift provided immediate feedback on how design, rigging, access, and field conditions interact.

title: Lavo Bot Prototype Development
subtitle: Hands-on prototype work for a high-productivity pressure-washing robot.
alt: Opened prototype pressure-washing robot showing large surface-cleaner assembly, wiring, motors, frame members, and controls.
tools: Mechanical fabrication, wiring, motors, controls, rapid iteration, hand and power tools
challenge: Develop and refine an early ground-based pressure-washing robot around a large surface-cleaner assembly while packaging drive, control, and fluid-handling components into a workable prototype.
approach: Contributed to prototype assembly, component placement, wiring, fit checks, and troubleshooting. The open architecture made it possible to test ideas quickly and revise the arrangement as the product concept evolved.
outcome: Supported early development work that helped turn a pressure-washing concept into a functional robotic platform.
learned: Prototype layouts should prioritize access, testability, and fast modification before final packaging.
Mechanical, electrical, and fluid systems have to be considered together from the beginning.
Hands-on prototype work exposes integration problems that are easy to miss in isolated component design.

title: AI Trading Decision System
subtitle: A modular paper-trading platform for scanning, scoring, risk control, and performance review.
alt: Dark engineering-style dashboard concept for an automated paper-trading system with scanner, positions, equity curve, and activity panels.
tools: Python, Alpaca API, automated logging, backtesting, replay testing, risk controls
challenge: Create a system that could evaluate a broad stock universe, separate stronger opportunities from weak setups, execute paper trades, and record enough information to improve the decision process.
approach: Developed modular scanning, scoring, order-management, error-recovery, and reporting logic. Added position limits, stale-order handling, end-of-day controls, cooldowns, and replay/backtest workflows. The interface shown is a concept visualization for the system's Mission Control dashboard.
outcome: Built a working paper-trading research environment that connects software architecture, data logic, execution safeguards, and performance analysis.
learned: A decision system is only as useful as its logging, failure handling, and ability to explain why an action occurred.
Backtesting must include realistic assumptions such as slippage, position limits, and unavailable fills.
Risk controls and system reliability are engineering requirements, not optional features.

title: Before the Recordable
subtitle: A near-miss reporting and analytics concept designed to reveal safety risk earlier.
alt: Presentation cover for Before the Recordable, a construction safety analytics capstone project.
role: Capstone research, solution design, and presentation
tools: QR workflow, dashboard concept, leading indicators, pilot planning, technical presentation
challenge: Find a practical way for a contractor to identify repeated hazards before they become recordable incidents.
approach: Designed a quick QR-based reporting flow: scan, report, analyze, and act. Proposed a 30-to-60-day pilot that tracks reporting time, repeated hazards, response time, corrective actions, and recurrence after action.
outcome: Created a realistic implementation concept that turns individual near misses into measurable leading indicators for project leadership and safety teams.
learned: Reporting has to be fast enough for field participation.
A dashboard is useful only when it drives visible corrective action.
Trust and a learning-focused culture are critical to collecting honest near-miss information.
